rule curation note: Encodes an intrinsic class D β-lactamase (OXA-55) capable of hydrolyzing carbapenems and other β-lactams. Frequently located on plasmids or mobile genetic elements, this gene confers resistance to ampicillin, carbapenems, and other β-lactam antibiotics in Shewanella spp.

Encodes an intrinsic class D β-lactamase (OXA-55) capable of hydrolyzing carbapenems and other β-lactams. Frequently located on plasmids or mobile genetic elements, this gene confers resistance to ampicillin, carbapenems, and other β-lactam antibiotics in Shewanella spp.

Encodes a sulfonamide-resistant dihydropteroate synthase, typically embedded within the 3′ conserved segment of class 1 integrons. Presence of sul1 confers resistance to sulfonamides such as sulfamethoxazole, often in combination with other resistance determinants.

A core housekeeping gene encoding the A subunit of DNA gyrase, essential for DNA replication. Point mutations in the quinolone resistance-determining region (QRDR), such as S83V, reduce fluoroquinolone binding and confer resistance to quinolones including ciprofloxacin.

The qnrA gene is identified as a core chromosomal component in S. algae (present in >95% of isolates). While qnrA homologs in Enterobacterales are well-documented to confer low-level quinolone resistance, current evidence suggests that in S. algae, this gene does not typically translate into a resistant phenotype. Its biological role in this species is hypothesized to be linked to physiological functions such as thermal adaptation rather than active antibiotic resistance. Therefore, while it is a genomic core feature, its presence alone should not be used as a definitive predictor of quinolone resistance in S. algae.
